Using Dataflow allows for continuous processing of incoming data in real-time, which is essential for aggregating events across disjoint hourly intervals as mentioned in the question. Tumbling windows in Dataflow can be used to aggregate data within specific time intervals, such as hourly intervals in this case.Now, let‘s explain why the other options are incorrect:A. Creating a Cloud Function to perform data processing on a Pub/Sub trigger every time a new message is published:– This option would not be ideal for aggregating events across hourly intervals as it triggers on each message published, which may not align with the hourly aggregation requirement. Additionally, scaling may become an issue with large volumes of events.B. Scheduling a Cloud Function to run hourly and pulling all messages from Pub/Sub for aggregations:– This option involves batch processing and is not suitable for real-time processing of events as required. It may not be able to handle large volumes of events efficiently.C. Scheduling a batch Dataflow job to run hourly for aggregations:– While using Dataflow is a good choice, scheduling a batch job to run hourly is not optimal for processing real-time events. It may not meet the requirement of aggregating events across disjoint hourly intervals in a continuous manner.

questions.question: Scenario: You are tasked with monitoring data pipelines on BigQuery, Dataflow, and Dataproc for health checks and failure notifications. You need to work across multiple projects and prefer using managed products or platform features. What steps should you take to fulfill these requirements effectively?
options: Export the information to Cloud Monitoring, and set up an Alerting policy
Run a Virtual Machine in Compute Engine with Airflow, and export the information to Cloud Monitoring
Export the logs to BigQuery, and set up App Engine to read that information and send emails if you find a failure in the logs
Develop an App Engine application to consume logs using GCP API calls, and send emails if you find a failure in the logs
answer: Export the information to Cloud Monitoring, and set up an Alerting policy
questions.explanation: A. Export the information to Cloud Monitoring, and set up an Alerting policy– Exporting the information to Cloud Monitoring is the correct approach for monitoring the health and behavior of data pipelines running on BigQuery, Dataflow, and Dataproc. Cloud Monitoring is a managed service that allows you to monitor and manage your GCP resources effectively.– By setting up an Alerting policy in Cloud Monitoring, you can define conditions based on metrics or logs and receive notifications when those conditions are met. This way, you can notify the team managing the pipelines if any failures are detected.Why the other options are incorrect:B. Running a Virtual Machine in Compute Engine with Airflow and exporting the information to Cloud Monitoring is not the optimal solution as it involves managing infrastructure and does not leverage the managed services provided by GCP for monitoring purposes.C. Exporting the logs to BigQuery and setting up App Engine to read the information and send emails if a failure is detected is a more complex and less efficient approach compared to using Cloud Monitoring for monitoring and alerting.D. Developing an App Engine application to consume logs using GCP API calls and send emails in case of failures is a custom solution that requires more effort and maintenance compared to using Cloud Monitoring‘s built-in alerting capabilities.

questions.question: Scenario: You are rebuilding your batch pipeline for structured data on Google Cloud using PySpark for data transformations. The pipelines are currently taking over twelve hours to run, so you want to expedite development and reduce run time by using a serverless tool and SOL syntax. Your raw data has been moved to Cloud Storage. How can you construct the pipeline on Google Cloud to ensure speed and processing requirements are met?
options: Convert your PySpark commands into SparkSQL queries to transform the data, and then run your pipeline on Dataproc to write the data into BigQuery.
Ingest your data into Cloud SQL, convert your PySpark commands into SparkSQL queries to transform the data, and then use federated quenes from BigQuery for machine learning.
Ingest your data into BigQuery from Cloud Storage, convert your PySpark commands into BigQuery SQL queries to transform the data, and then write the transformations to a new table.
Use Apache Beam Python SDK to build the transformation pipelines, and write the data into BigQuery.
answer: Ingest your data into BigQuery from Cloud Storage, convert your PySpark commands into BigQuery SQL queries to transform the data, and then write the transformations to a new table.
questions.explanation: The correct answer is C.In this scenario, to expedite development and pipeline run time, you should use a serverless tool and SQL syntax. In this context, ingesting your data directly into BigQuery from Cloud Storage, converting your PySpark commands into BigQuery SQL queries to transform the data, and then writing the transformations to a new table is the most efficient approach.Now, let‘s discuss why the other options are incorrect:A. Converting PySpark commands into SparkSQL queries and running the pipeline on Dataproc to write the data into BigQuery may not provide the desired speed improvements. Dataproc is a managed Spark and Hadoop service that requires cluster management, which can introduce overhead and longer processing times compared to serverless options like BigQuery.B. Ingesting data into Cloud SQL and using federated queries from BigQuery for machine learning introduces unnecessary complexity and potential performance bottlenecks. Cloud SQL may not be the ideal solution for large-scale data processing compared to BigQuery‘s serverless architecture.D. While using Apache Beam Python SDK to build transformation pipelines is a valid option, writing the data into BigQuery directly from PySpark commands converted to BigQuery SQL queries would be more efficient in terms of speed and processing requirements.

questions.question: Scenario: You are evaluating a Dataflow pipeline designed to process compressed gzip text files, handle errors by writing them to a dead-letter queue, and utilize SideInputs for data joining. What steps can you take to speed up the completion of the Dataflow job that is running slower than anticipated?
options: Switch to compressed Avro files.
Reduce the batch size.
Retry records that throw an error.
Use CoGroupByKey instead of the SideInput.
answer: Use CoGroupByKey instead of the SideInput.
questions.explanation: The correct answer is D. Use CoGroupByKey instead of the SideInput.When using SideInputs in Dataflow, it can sometimes lead to longer processing times due to the overhead of managing the side data. CoGroupByKey is a more efficient alternative in certain scenarios because it allows for a more optimized processing of data across multiple collections without the need for side inputs. By using CoGroupByKey instead of SideInput, you can potentially expedite the Dataflow job.Now, let‘s explain why the other options are incorrect:A. Switch to compressed Avro files:Switching to compressed Avro files may help reduce the file sizes and improve read performance, but it may not directly address the issue of longer processing times in the Dataflow job. This option may not be as effective in expediting the job compared to using CoGroupByKey.B. Reduce the batch size:Reducing the batch size can sometimes help improve the processing time by allowing for more frequent updates and smaller chunks of data to be processed. However, this may not be as effective as using CoGroupByKey to optimize the processing logic.C. Retry records that throw an error:Retrying records that throw an error can help improve data reliability and integrity, but it may not directly address the issue of longer processing times. This option may not be as effective in expediting the Dataflow job compared to using CoGroupByKey.In conclusion, using CoGroupByKey instead of SideInput is the most appropriate solution to potentially expedite the Dataflow job in this scenario.

questions.question: Scenario: You are migrating your data warehouse to Google Cloud and decommissioning your on-premises data center. Bandwidth will be allocated for the initial data load to the cloud. The files are not numerous, but each file is 90 GB. You also need your transactional systems to update the warehouse on Google Cloud in real time. Which tools should you utilize for data migration and to ensure real-time updates to your warehouse on Google Cloud?
options: Storage Transfer Service for the migration; Pub/Sub and Cloud Data Fusion for the real-time updates
BigQuery Data Transfer Service for the migration; Pub/Sub and Dataproc for the real-time updates
gsutil for the migration; Pub/Sub and Dataflow for the real-time updates
gsutil for both the migration and the real-time updates
answer: gsutil for the migration; Pub/Sub and Dataflow for the real-time updates
questions.explanation: The correct answer is C.– gsutil for the migration: ′gsutil′ is a command-line tool that allows you to manage Cloud Storage resources. It is a reliable and efficient tool for transferring large files to Google Cloud Storage. Since the files being transferred are large (90 GB each), ′gsutil′ is a suitable choice for this task.– Pub/Sub and Dataflow for the real-time updates:– Pub/Sub: Cloud Pub/Sub is a messaging service that allows you to send and receive messages between independent applications. In this scenario, Pub/Sub can be used to stream real-time updates from your transactional systems to your data warehouse on Google Cloud.– Dataflow: Cloud Dataflow is a fully managed service for processing and enriching real-time data streams. It provides a scalable and reliable way to process and analyze streaming data. By using Dataflow, you can ensure that your transactional systems continually update the warehouse on Google Cloud in real time.Now, let‘s discuss why the other options are incorrect:A. Storage Transfer Service for the migration; Pub/Sub and Cloud Data Fusion for the real-time updates:– Storage Transfer Service is primarily used for transferring data from on-premises storage to Cloud Storage. Since you are decommissioning your on-premises data center, Storage Transfer Service is not necessary in this scenario.– Cloud Data Fusion is a data integration service that allows you to build and manage ETL (extract, transform, load) pipelines. While it can be used for real-time data processing, Dataflow is a better fit for this specific use case of continuously updating a data warehouse in real time.B. BigQuery Data Transfer Service for the migration; Pub/Sub and Dataproc for the real-time updates:– BigQuery Data Transfer Service is used for automating data transfers from SaaS applications to BigQuery. Since you are migrating data from your on-premises data center to Google Cloud Storage, BigQuery Data Transfer Service is not the appropriate tool for this task.– Dataproc is a managed Spark and Hadoop service that can be used for processing large datasets. While Dataproc can be used for real-time data processing, Dataflow is better suited for streaming data processing and is the recommended tool for this use case.D. gsutil for both the migration and the real-time updates:– While ′gsutil′ is suitable for the initial data migration to Google Cloud Storage, it is not designed for real-time updates. Pub/Sub and Dataflow are better choices for streaming real-time data updates to your data warehouse.

questions.question: Scenario: You are managing a streaming Cloud Dataflow pipeline. Your team has developed a new version of the pipeline with a changed windowing algorithm and triggering strategy. How can you update the current pipeline to the new version without losing any data?
options: Update the Cloud Dataflow pipeline inflight by passing the --update option with the --jobName set to the existing job name
Update the Cloud Dataflow pipeline inflight by passing the --update option with the --jobName set to a new unique job name
Stop the Cloud Dataflow pipeline with the Cancel option. Create a new Cloud Dataflow job with the updated code
Stop the Cloud Dataflow pipeline with the Drain option. Create a new Cloud Dataflow job with the updated code
answer: Stop the Cloud Dataflow pipeline with the Drain option. Create a new Cloud Dataflow job with the updated code
questions.explanation: Correct Option D. Stop the Cloud Dataflow pipeline with the Drain option. Create a new Cloud Dataflow job with the updated code Correct because the Drain option allows the pipeline to finish processing all in-flight data before shutting down. This ensures that no data is lost when transitioning to the new pipeline version. After draining, you can safely deploy the updated pipeline with the new windowing and triggering strategy. Incorrect Option A. Update the Cloud Dataflow pipeline inflight with --update and existing job name Incorrect because the --update option only supports compatible changes (e.g., parameter updates, minor code changes). Major changes like windowing and triggering strategies are not supported by in-place updates. Attempting this would fail or cause inconsistent behavior. Option B. Update the Cloud Dataflow pipeline inflight with --update and a new unique job name Incorrect because using a new job name creates a new job entirely, not an update. This does not preserve the state of the existing pipeline and may lead to data loss since the old pipeline is not drained properly. Option C. Stop the Cloud Dataflow pipeline with the Cancel option and create a new job Incorrect because Cancel immediately terminates the pipeline, discarding any in-flight data. This results in data loss, which violates the requirement of preserving all data during the update.

questions.question: Scenario: Monthly, you receive data files in CSV format from a third party. The data needs to be cleansed, but every third month, the file schema changes. Your implementation requirements include executing transformations on schedule, allowing non-developer analysts to make changes to transformations, and offering a graphical tool for designing transformations. What action should you take in this situation?
options: Use Dataprep by Trifacta to build and maintain the transformation recipes, and execute them on a scheduled basis
Load each month‘s CSV data into BigQuery, and write a SQL query to transform the data to a standard schema. Merge the transformed tables together with a SQL query
Help the analysts write a Dataflow pipeline in Python to perform the transformation. The Python code should be stored in a revision control system and modified as the incoming data‘s schema changes

Use Apache Spark on Dataproc to infer the schema of the CSV file before creating a Dataframe. Then implement the transformations in Spark SQL before writing the data out to Cloud Storage and loading into BigQuery
answer: Use Dataprep by Trifacta to build and maintain the transformation recipes, and execute them on a scheduled basis
questions.explanation: A. Use Dataprep by Trifacta to build and maintain the transformation recipes, and execute them on a scheduled basis.– Dataprep by Trifacta is a tool specifically designed for data preparation and transformation. It provides a graphical interface that enables non-developer analysts to easily design and modify transformations.– With Dataprep, you can build and maintain transformation recipes to cleanse the data. The tool allows for scheduling the transformations to be executed on a regular basis, which meets the requirement of executing the transformations on a schedule.– The graphical tool provided by Dataprep makes it easy for non-developer analysts to modify the transformations, fulfilling the requirement of enabling analysts to modify transformations without requiring deep technical knowledge.Why the other options are incorrect:B. Loading each month‘s CSV data into BigQuery and writing a SQL query for transformation:– This approach does not provide a graphical tool for designing transformations, which is one of the requirements. It also does not enable non-developer analysts to easily modify the transformations.C. Helping the analysts write a Dataflow pipeline in Python:– This option requires writing Python code for the transformations, which may not be suitable for non-developer analysts. It also does not provide a graphical tool for designing transformations.D. Using Apache Spark on Dataproc to infer the schema and implement transformations:– This option involves more technical complexity and may not be suitable for non-developer analysts to modify the transformations. It also does not provide a graphical tool for designing transformations as required.

questions.question: Scenario: You are migrating an on-premises Hadoop system to Cloud Dataproc. Hive is the primary tool in use, and the data format is Optimized Row Columnar (ORC). All ORC files have been successfully copied to a Cloud Storage bucket. You need to replicate some data to the cluster‘s local Hadoop Distributed File System (HDFS) to maximize performance. What are two ways to start using Hive in Cloud Dataproc? (Choose two.)
options: Run the gsutil utility to transfer all ORC files from the Cloud Storage bucket to HDFS. Mount the Hive tables locally.
Run the gsutil utility to transfer all ORC files from the Cloud Storage bucket to any node of the Dataproc cluster. Mount the Hive tables locally.

Run the gsutil utility to transfer all ORC files from the Cloud Storage bucket to the master node of the Dataproc cluster. Then run the Hadoop utility to copy them do HDFS. Mount the Hive tables from HDFS.
Leverage Cloud Storage connector for Hadoop to mount the ORC files as external Hive tables. Replicate external Hive tables to the native ones.
Load the ORC files into BigQuery. Leverage BigQuery connector for Hadoop to mount the BigQuery tables as external Hive tables. Replicate external Hive tables to the native ones.
answer: Run the gsutil utility to transfer all ORC files from the Cloud Storage bucket to the master node of the Dataproc cluster. Then run the Hadoop utility to copy them do HDFS. Mount the Hive tables from HDFS.
Leverage Cloud Storage connector for Hadoop to mount the ORC files as external Hive tables. Replicate external Hive tables to the native ones.
questions.explanation: The correct answers are C and D.C. Running the gsutil utility to transfer all ORC files from the Cloud Storage bucket to the master node of the Dataproc cluster and then running the Hadoop utility to copy them to HDFS is a valid approach. This method allows you to copy the data to HDFS on the Dataproc cluster and then mount the Hive tables from HDFS.D. Leveraging the Cloud Storage connector for Hadoop to mount the ORC files as external Hive tables is another correct approach. By using the Cloud Storage connector, you can directly access the ORC files stored in Cloud Storage as external Hive tables. Replicating external Hive tables to native ones allows you to work with the data efficiently within the Cloud Dataproc environment.Explanation of Incorrect Options:A. Running the gsutil utility to transfer all ORC files from the Cloud Storage bucket to HDFS and then mounting the Hive tables locally is not the best approach. This method involves unnecessary data movement and does not leverage the capabilities of Cloud Dataproc efficiently.B. Running the gsutil utility to transfer all ORC files from the Cloud Storage bucket to any node of the Dataproc cluster and then mounting the Hive tables locally is not recommended. Directly transferring files to a single node may not ensure optimal data availability and performance across the cluster.E. Loading the ORC files into BigQuery and leveraging the BigQuery connector for Hadoop to mount the BigQuery tables as external Hive tables is not the most suitable approach for migrating an on-premises Hadoop system to Cloud Dataproc. While BigQuery integration is useful for certain use cases, in this scenario, transferring data to HDFS and using Hive in Cloud Dataproc would be more appropriate.

questions.question: Scenario: To build a managed Hadoop system for your data lake, the data transformation process involves a sequence of Hadoop jobs. You have opted to use the Cloud Storage connector to store input, output, and intermediary data, separating storage from compute. However, you have observed that a specific Hadoop job runs significantly slower on Cloud Dataproc compared to an on-premises bare-metal Hadoop environment with 8-core nodes and 100-GB RAM, indicating disk I/O intensity. How can you address the slow performance of the disk I/O intensive Hadoop job on Cloud Dataproc compared to the on-premises environment?
options: Allocate sufficient memory to the Hadoop cluster, so that the intermediary data of that particular Hadoop job can be held in memory
Allocate sufficient persistent disk space to the Hadoop cluster, and store the intermediate data of that particular Hadoop job on native HDFS
Allocate more CPU cores of the virtual machine instances of the Hadoop cluster so that the networking bandwidth for each instance can scale up
Allocate additional network interface card (NIC), and configure link aggregation in the operating system to use the combined throughput when working with Cloud Storage
answer: Allocate sufficient persistent disk space to the Hadoop cluster, and store the intermediate data of that particular Hadoop job on native HDFS
questions.explanation: The correct answer is B. Allocate sufficient persistent disk space to the Hadoop cluster, and store the intermediate data of that particular Hadoop job on native HDFS.Explanation of why B is correct:– The particular Hadoop job is disk I/O intensive, meaning it heavily relies on reading and writing data to disk. By allocating sufficient persistent disk space to the Hadoop cluster and storing the intermediate data on native HDFS, you can improve the performance of the job. Native HDFS (Hadoop Distributed File System) is optimized for Hadoop jobs and can handle disk I/O operations efficiently.Explanation of why the other options are incorrect:A. Allocating sufficient memory to hold the intermediary data in memory may help in some cases, but if the job is disk I/O intensive, storing data in memory may not be the most efficient solution as it could lead to memory constraints and performance issues.C. Allocating more CPU cores to scale up networking bandwidth may improve overall cluster performance but may not directly address the disk I/O issue of the specific Hadoop job. Increasing CPU cores may not necessarily alleviate disk I/O bottlenecks.D. Adding additional network interface cards and configuring link aggregation may improve network throughput, but it does not directly address the disk I/O issue of the slow Hadoop job. Network bandwidth improvements may not significantly impact the performance of a disk I/O intensive job.

questions.question: Scenario: Operating an IoT pipeline based on Apache Kafka with an average of 5000 messages per second, you aim to set up an alert on Google Cloud Platform for when the 1-hour moving average falls below 4000 messages per second. What steps should be taken to achieve this alert setup using Google Cloud Platform?
options: Consume the stream of data in Dataflow using Kafka IO. Set a sliding time window of 1 hour every 5 minutes. Compute the average when the window closes, and send an alert if the average is less than 4000 messages.
Consume the stream of data in Dataflow using Kafka IO. Set a fixed time window of 1 hour. Compute the average when the window closes, and send an alert if the average is less than 4000 messages.

Use Kafka Connect to link your Kafka message queue to Pub/Sub. Use a Dataflow template to write your messages from Pub/Sub to Bigtable. Use Cloud Scheduler to run a script every hour that counts the number of rows created in Bigtable in the last hour. If that number falls below 4000, send an alert.

Use Kafka Connect to link your Kafka message queue to Pub/Sub. Use a Dataflow template to write your messages from Pub/Sub to BigQuery. Use Cloud Scheduler to run a script every five minutes that counts the number of rows created in BigQuery in the last hour. If that number falls below 4000, send an alert.
answer: Consume the stream of data in Dataflow using Kafka IO. Set a sliding time window of 1 hour every 5 minutes. Compute the average when the window closes, and send an alert if the average is less than 4000 messages.
questions.explanation: A. Consume the stream of data in Dataflow using Kafka IO. Set a sliding time window of 1 hour every 5 minutes. Compute the average when the window closes, and send an alert if the average is less than 4000 messages.– This option is correct because it uses Dataflow with Kafka IO to consume the stream of data. It sets a sliding time window of 1 hour every 5 minutes, which allows for near-real-time monitoring of the moving average over 1 hour.– By computing the average when the window closes, it ensures that the alert is based on accurate and recent data.– Sending an alert if the average is less than 4000 messages meets the requirement of creating an alert as soon as the moving average drops below 4000 messages per second.Now, let‘s explain why the other options are incorrect:B. Consume the stream of data in Dataflow using Kafka IO. Set a fixed time window of 1 hour. Compute the average when the window closes, and send an alert if the average is less than 4000 messages.– This option uses a fixed time window of 1 hour, which does not provide the granularity needed for near-real-time monitoring of the moving average. It may not detect drops in the message rate as quickly as required.C. Use Kafka Connect to link your Kafka message queue to Pub/Sub. Use a Dataflow template to write your messages from Pub/Sub to Bigtable. Use Cloud Scheduler to run a script every hour that counts the number of rows created in Bigtable in the last hour. If that number falls below 4000, send an alert.– This option involves unnecessary steps by writing messages to Bigtable and counting rows. It does not directly address the requirement of monitoring the message rate from Kafka.D. Use Kafka Connect to link your Kafka message queue to Pub/Sub. Use a Dataflow template to write your messages from Pub/Sub to BigQuery. Use Cloud Scheduler to run a script every five minutes that counts the number of rows created in BigQuery in the last hour. If that number falls below 4000, send an alert.– Similar to option C, this approach involves unnecessary steps with BigQuery and counting rows, which complicates the monitoring process and does not directly address the requirement of monitoring the message rate from Kafka.In conclusion, Option A is the most suitable choice as it leverages Dataflow with Kafka IO to efficiently monitor the moving average of messages per second and trigger alerts when needed.

questions.question: Scenario: Your company is in the process of choosing a system for centralizing data ingestion and delivery. The options being considered include messaging and data integration systems that can meet specific requirements. Given the key requirements of seeking to a particular offset in a topic, support for publish/subscribe on multiple topics, and retaining per-key ordering, which system would be the most suitable choice?
options: Apache Kafka
Cloud Storage
Dataflow
Firebase Cloud Messaging
answer: Apache Kafka
questions.explanation: Apache Kafka (Option A) is the correct choice for this scenario. Here‘s why:1. Ability to seek to a particular offset in a topic: Kafka allows consumers to seek to a specific offset within a topic, enabling them to read messages from that point onwards. This feature aligns with the requirement of seeking to a particular offset in a topic, even back to the start of all data ever captured.2. Support for publish/subscribe semantics on hundreds of topics: Kafka is designed for high-throughput, fault-tolerant, and real-time data streaming. It can handle the publish/subscribe model efficiently across hundreds of topics, making it suitable for the requirement of supporting publish/subscribe semantics on multiple topics.3. Retain per-key ordering: Kafka retains the order of messages within a partition. This means that messages with the same key will always be stored in the same partition and in the order they were received. This feature ensures that Kafka maintains per-key ordering, which is a critical requirement in this scenario.Explanation of why the other options are incorrect:– Cloud Storage (Option B): While cloud storage solutions like Google Cloud Storage or Amazon S3 are suitable for storing large volumes of data, they do not provide the real-time data streaming capabilities and message retention features required in this scenario. Cloud Storage is not optimized for data ingestion and delivery with the specific requirements mentioned.– Dataflow (Option C): Google Cloud Dataflow is a fully managed stream and batch processing service, but it is more focused on data processing and transformation rather than being a dedicated messaging and data integration system like Kafka. Dataflow may not provide the same level of control over offsets and message ordering as Kafka does.– Firebase Cloud Messaging (Option D): Firebase Cloud Messaging is a cross-platform messaging solution for reliably delivering messages at no cost. However, it is primarily designed for sending notifications and messages to mobile devices and does not offer the same capabilities as Kafka for data ingestion, retention, and ordering.In summary, Apache Kafka is the most suitable choice for centralizing data ingestion and delivery in this scenario due to its robust messaging capabilities, support for seeking offsets, handling multiple topics with publish/subscribe semantics, and retaining per-key ordering of messages.

questions.question: Scenario: You are migrating your current on-premises Apache Hadoop deployment to the cloud to ensure fault-tolerance and cost-effectiveness for long-running batch jobs using a managed service. What steps should you take for migrating your Apache Hadoop deployment to the cloud to achieve fault-tolerance and cost-effectiveness for long-running batch jobs using a managed service?
options: Deploy a Dataproc cluster. Use a standard persistent disk and 50% preemptible workers. Store data in Cloud Storage, and change references in scripts from hdfs:// to gs://
Deploy a Dataproc cluster. Use an SSD persistent disk and 50% preemptible workers. Store data in Cloud Storage, and change references in scripts from hdfs:// to gs://

Install Hadoop and Spark on a 10-node Compute Engine instance group with standard instances. Install the Cloud Storage connector, and store the data in Cloud Storage. Change references in scripts from hdfs:// to gs://
Install Hadoop and Spark on a 10-node Compute Engine instance group with preemptible instances. Store data in HDFS. Change references in scripts from hdfs:// to gs://
answer: Deploy a Dataproc cluster. Use a standard persistent disk and 50% preemptible workers. Store data in Cloud Storage, and change references in scripts from hdfs:// to gs://
questions.explanation: A. Deploy a Dataproc cluster. Use a standard persistent disk and 50% preemptible workers. Store data in Cloud Storage, and change references in scripts from hdfs:// to gs://– Deploying a Dataproc cluster is a good choice as it is a managed service for running Apache Hadoop and Apache Spark jobs. This option provides fault tolerance and cost-effectiveness for long-running batch jobs.– Using a standard persistent disk is cost-effective compared to an SSD persistent disk, making it a suitable choice for storing data that doesn‘t require high-performance I/O.– Utilizing 50% preemptible workers helps reduce costs significantly as preemptible instances are cheaper but can be interrupted by Google Cloud.– Storing data in Cloud Storage is a good practice for scalable and durable storage, and changing references in scripts from hdfs:// to gs:// ensures seamless integration with Cloud Storage.Why the other options are incorrect:B. Deploying a Dataproc cluster with an SSD persistent disk might be unnecessary for batch jobs that do not require high-performance I/O, making it less cost-effective compared to using a standard persistent disk.C. Installing Hadoop and Spark on a 10-node Compute Engine instance group with standard instances is not a managed service like Dataproc, which means you will need to manage the infrastructure yourself. This option may not be as fault-tolerant, cost-effective, or scalable as using a managed service.D. Installing Hadoop and Spark on a 10-node Compute Engine instance group with preemptible instances can lead to potential interruptions since preemptible instances can be reclaimed by Google Cloud at any time. Storing data in HDFS can also be less cost-effective and scalable compared to using Cloud Storage.

questions.question: A consultant has recommended that you replace an existing messaging system with Cloud Pub/Sub. You are concerned that your existing system has a different delivery guarantee than Cloud Pub/Sub. What kind of message deliver semantics does Cloud Pub/Sub guarantee?
options: Deliver at most or deliver at least depending on configuration of the subscription
Deliver at least once
Deliver at most once
Best effort but no guarantee
answer: Deliver at least once
questions.explanation: Cloud Pub/Sub has a deliver at least once guarantee. It does not have deliver at most once guarantee. It is possible the Cloud Pub/Sub could deliver a message more than once. See https://cloud.google.com/pubsub/docs/subscriber
